text:
HEMATOLOGY / MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
OUTPATIENT FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Restaging Assessment — After 4 Cycles Neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old West African woman with borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), pancreatic head and uncinate process, presenting today for restaging assessment and clinical review after completion of 4 cycles of modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX) in the neoadjuvant setting.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu was originally diagnosed with borderline resectable PDAC of the pancreatic head and uncinate process approximately 3 months ago. At diagnosis, cross-sectional imaging demonstrated a 3.8 x 3.2 cm hypoenhancing mass in the pancreatic head with tumor contact along the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) encompassing greater than 180 degrees of circumference and direct abutment of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) with partial luminal compromise. One peripancreatic lymph node measuring 1.2 cm was suspicious for involvement. PET-CT confirmed regional nodal FDG avidity and no distant metastases. EUS-guided fine needle biopsy confirmed moderately differentiated infiltrating ductal adenocarcinoma (grade 2). Staging: T4 N1 M0, stage III per AJCC 8th edition. Germline ATM pathogenic variant (heterozygous, p.V2716fs frameshift) was identified on reflex germline testing prompted by the somatic NGS findings; BRCA2 splice site variant detected only at low VAF on tissue NGS was not confirmed on germline testing. CA 19-9 at diagnosis: 1,247 U/mL. CEA: 8.4 ng/mL. Performance status ECOG 1.

Following multidisciplinary tumor board review, the consensus recommendation was for neoadjuvant systemic chemotherapy with mFOLFIRINOX given good performance status, adequate end-organ function, and the goal of maximizing the chance of margin-negative (R0) resection by downsizing the tumor and its vascular involvement prior to surgical consideration. She was started on mFOLFIRINOX — oxali 85 mg/m2, iri 150 mg/m2 (dose-reduced at cycle start from the standard 180 mg/m2 given her age of 66 and underlying diabetic neuropathy risk), leucovorin 400 mg/m2, and 5FU (5-FU) 2400 mg/m2 as a 46-hour continuous infusion without bolus 5-FU. Pegfilgrastim was administered after each cycle. She tolerated cycle 1 well.

During cycle 2, she developed grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy (bilateral distal paresthesias of hands and feet, classic cold-sensitivity typical of oxali), grade 2 fatigue, and grade 2 diarrhea. Loperamide was initiated for diarrhea management. Given the onset and severity of sensory neuropathy, oxali was dose-reduced by 20% to 68 mg/m2 for subsequent cycles. Camptosar was maintained at 150 mg/m2. No febrile neutropenia occurred. CA 19-9 after cycle 2 was 620 U/mL, already representing a 50% decline from baseline.

She presents today for review of restaging CT imaging performed after completion of cycle 4 and for clinical and laboratory reassessment to determine how to proceed.

Overall, she reports feeling reasonably well given the circumstances. She notes continued mild paresthesias in her fingertips and toes, which she rates as a 3/10 in terms of bother and not significantly impacting fine motor tasks or ambulation. She continues to use loperamide as needed for intermittent loose stools, approximately 2-3 loose bowel movements per day, down from a peak of 4-5 at cycle 2. She reports moderate fatigue — she is able to perform her usual light household activities but tires more quickly than her baseline. She denies nausea or vomiting between cycles. She reports a decreased appetite but has maintained her weight approximately at baseline. She denies new abdominal pain, jaundice, or change in urine color. No fevers or chills. She endorses ongoing cold sensitivity in her hands.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Moderate fatigue (grade 2), stable appetite, no significant weight loss beyond expected, no fevers, no night sweats.
Neurologic: Bilateral distal sensory paresthesias, hands and feet, consistent with grade 2 peripheral neuropathy, stable since dose reduction of oxali after cycle 2. No motor weakness, no gait instability, no difficulty with buttoning or fine motor tasks.
Gastrointestinal: Mild intermittent diarrhea (2-3 loose stools/day, grade 1-2), improved from nadir with loperamide. No nausea or vomiting. No melena, hematochezia. No worsening abdominal pain. Mild early satiety.
Skin: No significant changes. No rash.
Musculoskeletal: No arthralgias or myalgias beyond baseline.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no edema.
Respiratory: No shortness of breath, no cough.
Genitourinary: No dysuria. Endorses polyuria, consistent with worsened glycemic control.
Endocrine: Patient and her primary care physician have noted some worsening of blood sugar control during chemotherapy; this is being addressed with her primary care provider.
Psychiatric: Coping reasonably well. Strong family support. Has engaged with the oncology social worker. Reports some anxiety about restaging results, as expected.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 132/78 mmHg; Heart rate 76 bpm, regular; Temperature 37.0°C; Respiratory rate 16/min; Oxygen saturation 98% on room air; Weight 59.8 kg (baseline 61.2 kg at diagnosis — approximately 1.4 kg below baseline).
Performance Status: ECOG 1.

General: Alert and oriented x3. Well-appearing. No acute distress. Appears age-stated. Mild fatigue evident but cooperative and communicative.
Head / Eyes / Ears / Nose / Throat: No icterus noted. Sclerae anicteric. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis.
Lymph Nodes: No palpable cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles or wheezes.
Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Mild residual fullness to deep palpation in the epigastrium and right upper quadrant, not frankly tender. No guarding or rigidity. No palpable hepatosplenomegaly. Bowel sounds present and normoactive.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves II–XII grossly intact. Diminished sensation to light touch in bilateral digits (fingertips and toes). Vibration sense mildly reduced at the great toes bilaterally. Proprioception intact. Strength 5/5 throughout all four extremities. Gait steady; no ataxia.
Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. Fingertips with mild subjective dysesthesia on exam; no trophic changes.
Skin: No jaundice. No rash or hand-foot changes.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

CA 19-9: 310 U/mL (markedly decreased from 1,247 U/mL at diagnosis, from 620 U/mL after cycle 2; representing a 75% decline from the diagnostic baseline)
CEA: 4.8 ng/mL (improved from 8.4 ng/mL at diagnosis)
CBC: WBC 4.8 x10^9/L; ANC 2.7 x10^9/L; Hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL (mild anemia, consistent with chemotherapy-related); Platelets 188 x10^9/L
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Sodium 138 mEq/L; Potassium 3.9 mEq/L; Creatinine 0.82 mg/dL; BUN 14 mg/dL; Glucose 178 mg/dL (mildly elevated, consistent with known diabetes); Total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL; Direct bilirubin 0.2 mg/dL; AST 28 U/L; ALT 31 U/L; Alkaline phosphatase 94 U/L; Albumin 3.6 g/dL
Magnesium: 1.8 mEq/L (low-normal; supplementation recommended)
HbA1c (recent): 8.6% (suboptimally controlled; discussed with patient; to follow up with PCP for insulin adjustment)
INR: 1.1; PTT: 29 seconds

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Restaging multiphasic CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (pancreatic protocol) was performed after cycle 4 of mFOLFIRINOX. Reviewed in detail with the radiology team.

Key findings: The primary pancreatic head/uncinate mass has clearly decreased in size, now measuring 3.1 x 2.5 cm, down from 3.8 x 3.2 cm at diagnosis. This represents a meaningful response — approximately a 22% decrease in the sum of longest diameters. The overall bulk of the tumor has softened, with the desmoplastic halo around the mass appearing somewhat less dense and confluent. Most importantly from a surgical standpoint, the degree of SMA contact has improved substantially: the tumor now contacts the SMA over approximately 90 to 120 degrees of circumference, down from greater than 180 degrees at diagnosis. This shift toward the 90-degree threshold is clinically very encouraging and suggests potential resectability may be achievable with continued treatment. The SMV demonstrates residual soft tissue contact, but the luminal caliber has improved and there is no longer meaningful luminal compromise. The previously suspicious peripancreatic lymph node has decreased to 0.7 cm and is no longer clearly abnormal. The main pancreatic duct dilation has partially improved (4 mm, down from 6 mm). No new hepatic lesions. No peritoneal implants or ascites. No pulmonary nodules on the lower chest cuts. Overall, this scan documents a partial radiographic response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. BORDERLINE RESECTABLE PANCREATIC DUCTAL ADENOCARCINOMA, PANCREATIC HEAD AND UNCINATE PROCESS — RESPONDING TO NEOADJUVANT mFOLFIRINOX

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is showing an excellent and encouraging response to neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX after 4 cycles. The CA 19-9 trajectory is particularly gratifying — a 75% decline from 1,247 to 310 U/mL is a strong marker of systemic disease response and favorable biology. This, combined with the clear reduction in tumor dimensions and significant improvement in SMA contact on cross-sectional imaging, indicates that the neoadjuvant approach is working. The SMA now appears to be contacted over roughly 90-120 degrees — still borderline, but meaningfully improved from the >180 degrees at presentation that rendered her initially unresectable by conventional criteria.

Plan: I had a detailed discussion with the patient today regarding these results. She was pleased and cautiously optimistic upon hearing about the response. We discussed that while these are encouraging signs, the tumor has not yet reached a point where the surgical team would feel confident proceeding directly to the operating room, and continued treatment is advisable.

Plan is to continue mFOLFIRINOX for 4 additional cycles (cycles 5 through 8), maintaining the dose modifications already in place (Eloxatin 68 mg/m2, Camptosar 150 mg/m2, no bolus 5-FU). After completion of 8 total cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy, we will obtain repeat restaging imaging and multidisciplinary tumor board reassessment for surgical candidacy. The ultimate goal remains R0 resection if the vascular anatomy permits.

2. Eloxatin-RELATED PERIPHERAL SENSORY NEUROPATHY — GRADE 2, STABLE

Her grade 2 neuropathy has remained stable following the 20% oxali dose reduction after cycle 2. She tolerates her current activities of daily living and ambulation without difficulty. We discussed that cumulative oxali exposure remains a concern — ongoing monitoring is essential. If neuropathy worsens to grade 3 or becomes functionally disabling, we will need to consider discontinuing oxali and converting to a modified regimen. For now, plan is to continue with close monitoring at each cycle visit. She understands the importance of reporting any new or worsening symptoms. Magnesium supplementation prescribed given low-normal level (magnesium oxide 400 mg daily).

3. DIARRHEA — GRADE 1, IMPROVED

Her diarrhea has improved to grade 1 with loperamide as needed. No dose modification for Camptosar is required at this time. She is reminded to stay well-hydrated and to continue PRN loperamide as needed. She is aware to contact us if she develops more than 4-6 loose stools per day or if she develops fever, as that would prompt urgent evaluation.

4. FATIGUE — GRADE 2, MANAGED CONSERVATIVELY

Mild-to-moderate fatigue is expected and noted. She is counseled on gentle activity (walking program), prioritizing sleep, and optimizing nutrition. No dose delay is needed at present.

5. ANEMIA — MILD, CONSISTENT WITH CHEMOTHERAPY

Hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL, asymptomatic. No intervention required at this time. Will recheck CBC with next cycle visit. Iron studies to be checked if hemoglobin drops further.

6. DIABETES MANAGEMENT — SUBOPTIMALLY CONTROLLED

HbA1c 8.6%, blood glucose 178 mg/dL in clinic today. Patient's glycemic control is worsened in the setting of stress, chemotherapy, and possibly steroid-containing antiemetic regimens. She is on metformin at baseline; however, given chemotherapy-related GI stress and current diarrhea, metformin may need to be held or adjusted pending discussion with her primary care physician. Patient has been in contact with endocrinology co-management. We will coordinate with the endocrinology team to optimize insulin/oral agent regimen. Notably, her pancreatic mass in the head and uncinate process may itself be contributing to endocrine dysfunction given its anatomic location.

7. GERMLINE ATM HETEROZYGOUS PATHOGENIC VARIANT

Germline testing completed; ATM p.V2716fs frameshift confirmed as germline heterozygous pathogenic variant. The patient was counseled at length about the implications of this finding for her own health (associations with breast, pancreatic, and potentially other cancers) and for first-degree family members. She understands that her children should be referred to genetic counseling for consideration of germline testing. ATM-targeted strategies (e.g., PARP inhibitors, ATR inhibitors) may have relevance in the future depending on clinical course, but are not incorporated into the current treatment plan given her ongoing neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX course. BRCA2 low-VAF variant was not confirmed on germline testing; per genetic counseling team, this is consistent with a somatic subclonal finding rather than a germline alteration. This distinction is important from a family counseling perspective.

8. NUTRITIONAL STATUS

Patient has lost approximately 1.4 kg since diagnosis. She has no significant cachexia at this point. She is enrolled with the oncology dietitian for nutritional counseling. She is using high-calorie protein supplements (2 cans/day). We will continue to monitor weight at each visit.

9. SOCIAL AND PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT

Patient continues to be followed by the oncology social worker and reports adequate family support from her adult children. She remains engaged and motivated. She has been connected with a pancreatic cancer patient support group. Mental health resources reviewed.

RETURN TO CLINIC:
Return in approximately 2 weeks for cycle 5 of mFOLFIRINOX. Labs to be drawn prior to visit (CBC with differential, CMP, CA 19-9, CEA). Repeat restaging imaging to be planned after completion of cycle 8. Patient instructed to call with any febrile episodes, severe diarrhea, worsening abdominal pain, jaundice, or significant neurologic change.

Time spent in face-to-face counseling and coordination of care: approximately 45 minutes.
Physician attestation: I personally reviewed the CT images with the radiologist, reviewed all laboratory results, examined the patient, and formulated the above plan.

Cancer Status: Present — responding to neoadjuvant therapy.
Intent of therapy: Neoadjuvant, curative intent.
